Grilled Spiced Fish with Sambal and Rice
hindi: सुंडा शैली की ग्रिल्ड मछली चावल के साथ

food_subgroup: Grilled fish with rice and condiments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ikan Bakar Sunda. FOODON_03302051 (grilled fish food product) is the closest defensible parent. The rice and sambal accompaniments are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, grilled) is the closest parent. The Sundanese spice marinade, sambal, and rice components are not individually represented in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Ikan Bakar Sunda with Rice as a composite dish.

main_ingredients: Sundanese spice marinade (turmeric, galangal, lemongrass, shallots, garlic, chilli)

serving.description: 1 plate (grilled fish portion with steamed rice and sambal)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 200 g of grilled fish, 200 g of steamed white rice, and 50 g of sambal and fresh vegetable sides.

nutrient_source.title: Ikan Bakar (Grilled Fish) and Steamed White Rice — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for grilled fish and cooked white rice, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for tilapia (grilled) and cooked long-grain white rice
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for Ikan Bakar Sunda with Rice as a combined dish. Values are estimated from HPB Foodo grilled fish entries and cooked white rice entries, weighted for a 200 g fish / 200 g rice / 50 g sambal-and-sides serving composition, then back-calculated to per-100 g. Sambal contribution to macros is minor and estimated from USDA SR Legacy chilli-based condiment data. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (steamed, Asian-style) GI ~64–72; composite dish GI estimated at ~56 after fish and sambal dilution effect
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ikan Bakar Sunda with Rice as a composite dish. The GI is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database value for steamed white rice (GI ~64–72, medium) as the primary glycemic driver, adjusted downward to approximately 56 to account for the protein and fat content of the grilled fish component, which slows gastric emptying and attenuates the glycemic response. This is consistent with published mixed-meal GI estimation methodology (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021). GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 41.4) / 100 ≈ 23.2, rounded to 23.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from sambal, marinade, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Estimated sodium of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (≈1,395 mg per full serving) approaches or exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium limit of 800 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation and limit sambal portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by steamed white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 56 and GL of approximately 23 per serving indicate a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion to half or substituting with brown rice to lower the glycemic load, while the grilled fish component provides beneficial lean protein that supports glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean grilled fish provides omega-3 fatty acids; low saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled fish prepared with minimal oil contributes beneficial unsaturated fatty acids and lean protein with low saturated fat (~0.7 g per 100 g), supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern, though the sodium content from sambal and marinade should be moderated for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ikan Bakar Sunda with Rice is a Singaporean-served Indonesian dish originating from the Sundanese culinary tradition of West Java, Indonesia. 'Ikan bakar' literally means 'grilled fish' in Malay and Indonesian. The fish — typically tilapia, snapper, or pomfret — is marinated in a fragrant Sundanese spice paste comprising turmeric, galangal, lemongrass, shallots, garlic, and chilli, then grilled over charcoal or an open flame until the exterior is lightly charred and smoky while the flesh remains moist and flaky. It is served with steamed white rice, a fiery sambal (chilli-based condiment), and fresh vegetable accompaniments such as cucumber, tomato, and raw cabbage. The dish is characterised by its bold, aromatic, and slightly spicy flavour profile with a smoky char note. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density of approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with lean protein from the fish (~11 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from the rice (~18 g per 100 g), and low saturated fat (~0.7 g per 100 g). The estimated glycemic index is 56 (medium), driven primarily by the steamed white rice component. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most dietary patterns but warrants sodium monitoring due to sambal and marinade contributions; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate rice portion size to manage glycemic load. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it accessible across multiple dietary requirements common in Singapore's multicultural population.
common_user_queries: Calories in ikan bakar with rice
Is ikan bakar good for diabetics?
Grilled fish with sambal and rice calories

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Ikan Bakar Sunda with Rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo grilled fish and cooked white rice entries, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data, and weighted for a representative serving composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for steamed white rice adjusted for mixed-meal protein-fat dilution effect. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates for a 200 g fish / 200 g rice / 50 g sambal-and-sides serving, back-calculated to per 100 g. Actual restaurant portions may vary. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as grilled fish and plain rice contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy data. 3. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 4. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g is approximate; sambal sodium contribution varies significantly by recipe and restaurant. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all B-vitamins are estimated from USDA SR Legacy tilapia (grilled) and cooked white rice component data and should be treated as indicative rather than precise. 6. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Grains and staples'; this has been corrected to 'Fish and seafood' as the primary protein component is grilled fish, with rice as an accompaniment. 7. Served at Dapur Penyet - Funan, Singapore (field survey data source, 1 restaurant).
